data.title: Apple App Store Revenue Jumps 10%, Analyst Sees AI, App Diversification Driving Long-Term Growth
data.description:
Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) App Store delivered strong results in the September quarter, with revenue climbing 10% year-over-year to $8.7 billion and downloads rising 2.5% to 9 billion.

AAPL stock is up today. Get the details here.

The company also saw stronger monetization and a shift in revenue mix, as gaming's share declined while categories such as Productivity and Photo & Video posted steady growth, signaling healthier long-term trends alongside rising demand for artificial intelligence-powered apps.

BofA Securities analyst Wamsi Mohan maintained a Buy rating on Apple with a price target of $270.

Also Read: Apple Services Revenue Trajectory Signals Softness With Weaker App Store Downloads: Analyst

Mohan highlighted Apple’s strength in the App Store during the September quarter, including robust revenue growth, a healthier mix shift, and promising long-term trends tied to AI and app diversification.

The analyst noted App Store revenue rose 10% year-over-year in Apple’s fiscal fourth quarter of 2025, reaching $8.7 billion, according to developer revenue data from SensorTower. Total downloads across iPhone and iPad grew 2.5% year-over-year to nine billion during the same period, he noted.

Mohan emphasized that dollars per download increased 7.3% year-over-year to 97 cents, showing stronger monetization. For September alone, revenue rose 7% year-over-year, outpacing the 3% growth in downloads, the analyst said.

He cited strong capital returns, Apple’s eventual leadership in AI at the edge and optionality from new products and markets.

Mohan also noted the revenue mix continues to shift away from gaming, which bodes well for Apple’s long-term Services revenue. In the fourth quarter, games accounted for 45% of App Store revenue by category, down from historic levels above 50%, the analyst told. Productivity apps gained roughly 300 basis points year over year, the most significant growth among categories, he said.

While gaming remains the dominant contributor, Mohan viewed steady growth in categories such as Productivity and Photo & Video as constructive, since this diversification should support sustainable revenue growth. The analyst flagged Apple’s upcoming Oct. 21 appeal hearing in the Epic Games case as another key event for the App Store business.

On the AI front, he underscored how search-oriented AI apps are increasingly capturing daily active user share from the Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL)(NASDAQ:GOOG) Google search app (excluding Safari and Siri).

Google’s app share slipped to 73% in September, down from 85% in January, Mohan noted. ChatGPT emerged as the biggest beneficiary, with its share increasing from 14% in January to 24% in August, according to the analyst.

Mohan highlighted how ChatGPT has successfully monetized its user base, with App Store revenue rising from about $10 million in January 2024 to roughly $215 million in September 2025.

Mohan projected fiscal 2025 sales of $416.04 billion and EPS of $7.39.

AAPL Price Action: Apple shares were up 0.46% at $258.32 at publication on Friday. 

  • Apple’s iPhone 17 Impresses On Design, But Analysts Say AI Still Lags

Photo: Pandora Pictures via Shutterstock

data.title: From Buy To Hold: TD Securities Explains Why BlackBerry's QNX Growth Still Isn't Enough For A Bullish Call
data.description:
BlackBerry Ltd. (NYSE:BB) is drawing renewed attention from Wall Street as analysts reassess its prospects following momentum in its QNX automotive software business and stabilizing trends in secure communications.

The company is securing new contracts, expanding into adjacent markets, and benefiting from a recovering auto sector that is restarting delayed projects.

TD Securities analyst Daniel Chan resumed coverage of BlackBerry with a Hold rating, down from a Buy, and increased his price target to $5 from $4, citing operational progress but limited upside following the stock’s 61% rally since December 2024.

Chan pointed to easing macro pressures that helped boost demand for QNX developer licenses, driving about 15% growth last quarter. He emphasized QNX’s competitive strength, citing an eight-figure deal in Europe with a tier-1 automaker and added momentum in China.

He also said QNX’s success could extend to areas such as industrial automation, where BlackBerry has already landed a major contract.

Also Read: BlackBerry Stock Slips Despite Strong Q2, CEO Highlights Shift To System-Critical Software And Margin Gains

After divesting Cylance, Chan expects Secure Communications to stabilize, supported by government contracts. While net revenue retention remains at 93%, he views annual recurring revenue growth as a positive sign.

For fiscal 2026, Chan projects that Secure Communications revenue will decline 22% year over year, QNX revenue will increase 12%, and adjusted EBITDA will total $97 million.

Chan derived his $5 target by applying valuation multiples of 9.5x to 10.5x to calendar-2026 EBITDA for Secure Communications and 37.5x to 38.5x for QNX, reflecting a premium stance on its automotive software positioning. He concluded that the current share price already factors in much of the progress.

In a related note, BlackBerry’s stronger-than-expected Q2 results and raised guidance led analysts to revise their forecasts upward. The company posted EPS of $0.04 on revenue of $129.6 million, both ahead of estimates, and raised its full-year outlook to EPS of $0.11–$0.15 on revenue of $519–$541 million.

Baird raised its price target to $5.50 from $5 while maintaining a Neutral rating, and RBC Capital lifted its target to $4.50 from $4 with a Sector Perform rating, reflecting cautious optimism about BlackBerry’s near-term momentum.

Price Action: BB shares are trading lower by 3.89% at $4.56 at the last check on Friday.

Verizon, GE Launch Wireless Platform To Help Modernize America’s Grid

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: GameStop Stock Is Sliding Friday: What's Going On?
data.description:
GameStop Corp (NYSE:GME) shares are trading lower Friday morning. The company filed a prospectus with the Securities and Exchange Commission to sell an unspecified amount of various securities in the future. .

What To Know: The shelf registration, filed on October 2, allows the video game retailer to offer and sell securities such as Class A common stock, preferred stock, debt securities and warrants from time to time. This gives the company flexibility to raise capital more quickly when needed.

According to the filing, the net proceeds from any future sales would be used for general corporate purposes, which could include investments and potential acquisitions. The prospect of additional shares potentially entering the market, which could dilute existing shareholders’ value, has put downward pressure on the stock price in early trading.

Benzinga Edge Rankings: Despite the stock’s dip, Benzinga Edge stock rankings highlight an exceptionally strong Growth score of 99.15 for the company.

GME Price Action: GameStop shares were down 2.98% at $26.41 at the time of publication Friday, according to Benzinga Pro. The stock is trading within its 52-week range of $20.30 to $35.81.

The stock is above its 50-day moving average of $23.93, indicating a potential bullish trend, but it is below both the 100-day and 200-day moving averages at $25.06 and $25.85, respectively.

This positioning suggests that while short-term momentum may be positive, the longer-term trend remains uncertain, with key support likely around the 50-day moving average.

Read Also: Stock Market Today: Nasdaq, Dow Futures Gain On 3rd Day Of Shutdown

How To Buy GME Stock

Besides going to a brokerage platform to purchase a share – or fractional share – of stock, you can also gain access to shares either by buying an exchange traded fund (ETF) that holds the stock itself, or by allocating yourself to a strategy in your 401(k) that would seek to acquire shares in a mutual fund or other instrument.

For example, in GameStop’s case, it is in the Consumer Discretionary sector. An ETF will likely hold shares in many liquid and large companies that help track that sector, allowing an investor to gain exposure to the trends within that segment.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Safe Withdrawal Rates Aren't Static: How to Retire Smarter
data.description:
The most talked-about retirement rule of thumb just got a serious update. Bill Bengen, the financial planner who popularized the now-legendary "4% rule," has revisited his calculations. His latest research suggests retirees might safely withdraw closer to 4.7%—and perhaps even 5% or more—under the right conditions.

It's the kind of finding that makes soon-to-be retirees breathe a sigh of relief. But here's the catch: even this updated figure isn't meant to be a magic number. It's a guidepost. Real-world retirement isn't just about the math—it's about flexibility, timing, and personal goals.

Why the 4% Rule Became a Classic

When Bengen first introduced the rule in the 1990s, it was hailed as an elegant solution to a messy problem: how much can you safely withdraw from your retirement savings without running out of money?

The appeal was instant. If you could live off 4% of your portfolio annually, adjusted for inflation, you were considered financially independent. The calculation offered simplicity in a world of uncertainty.

But the simplicity was also the limitation. It assumed a 30-year retirement, a specific portfolio mix (U.S. stocks and bonds), and a willingness to spend down to zero. Over time, the 4% rule was treated more like gospel than what it really was: a historically informed estimate.

A Richer Framework

In his new book, A Richer Retirement, Bengen revisits his original analysis with a broader lens. Instead of limiting his work to three asset classes, he models seven, including international equities and real estate. He also factors in market valuations, inflation cycles, and different withdrawal strategies.

The result? A revised "Universal SAFEMAX" withdrawal rate of 4.7%, which he still considers conservative. But in certain market conditions, Bengen believes a rate as high as 5.25%–5.5% may be sustainable.

That's good news for retirees. But it also comes with a warning: history doesn't guarantee the future.

The Eight Elements That Matter

Bengen emphasizes that safe withdrawal rates aren't carved in stone—they depend on multiple factors, which he calls the "Eight Elements." Add to those inflation and market valuation, and you've got the ten key drivers of retirement sustainability.

Here are the Eight Elements to think about:

  1. Withdrawal method – Fixed-dollar withdrawals create stability, while inflation-adjusted ones protect purchasing power.
  2. Retirement horizon – Thirty years might not be enough anymore; many people live 35–40 years past retirement.
  3. Account type – Withdrawals from tax-deferred, Roth, or taxable accounts all carry different implications.
  4. Legacy goals – If you want to leave money behind, withdrawals must be lower.
  5. Asset allocation – The mix of stocks, bonds, real estate, and alternatives directly affects resilience.
  6. Rebalancing discipline – Adjusting portfolios back to their targets keeps risk in check.
  7. Management style – Passive indexing, active strategies, or alternatives all come with trade-offs.
  8. Withdrawal timing – Monthly, quarterly, or annual withdrawals may alter results, especially in volatile markets.

These elements prove one thing: withdrawal planning is a dynamic process, not a static number.

Inflation and Market Valuation: The Wild Cards

If there's one lesson from the past century, it's that inflation and market conditions at retirement matter more than any rule of thumb.

Take the 1970s, for instance. Retirees faced high inflation and poor returns—a toxic combination that wrecked even cautious plans. Inflation eroded purchasing power so quickly that even modest withdrawals weren't sustainable.

Today, the challenge may be different but no less concerning. Stock market valuations are historically elevated, which could mean lower-than-average returns ahead. Starting retirement in an overvalued market makes even 4.7% riskier than it looks on paper.

That's why Bengen warns: the SAFEMAX is "historically safe," not future-proof.

Why 4.7% Isn't Your Personal Number

The appeal of a higher withdrawal rate is obvious. More money for travel, hobbies, or simply peace of mind. But context matters.

Consider these two retirees:

  • Dominique wants to leave $500,000 to her children. To preserve her legacy, she chooses a cautious 3.5% withdrawal rate, treating any upside as a bonus.
  • Damien plans to front-load his retirement with travel. He starts closer to 5%, but he knows he'll need to scale back if markets disappoint.

Both strategies are valid because both reflect personal goals. The lesson: your "number" depends on your lifestyle, your priorities, and your willingness to adapt.

Guidepost, Not Goalpost

Here's the risk: treating a rule of thumb as if it were Newton's law. Bengen himself is clear on this point:

"The so-called 4% rule is not an immutable law, like Newton's Laws of Motion… I consider myself more as a reporter of what has occurred in the past than as a Nostradamus prophesying what will occur in the future."

In other words, treat the rule as a guidepost, not a goalpost. Use it to orient yourself, but don't let it box you into rigidity.

Flexibility: The True Retirement Superpower

The retirees who succeed financially aren't the ones who pick the "perfect" number—they're the ones who stay flexible.

  • Cut spending slightly during down markets.
  • Revisit your plan every year or two.
  • Adapt to changes in inflation, healthcare costs, and lifestyle choices.

Rigidity is what breaks retirement plans. Flexibility is what keeps them intact.

Tools to Navigate Retirement Planning

Bengen suggests retirees take advantage of modern tools that stress-test portfolios against different scenarios. Apps like Big Picture let you model different withdrawal rates, asset mixes, and time horizons. While no tool can predict the future, they help visualize risk and prepare you for contingencies.

Beyond software, retirees should maintain a disciplined review cycle. A withdrawal rate chosen at 65 shouldn't remain untouched at 75 or 85. Regular reviews, ideally with a financial advisor, turn theory into resilience.

The Bottom Line

The 4% rule changed the way we think about retirement. Now, decades later, it's getting a thoughtful upgrade. Bengen's research suggests that 4.7%—or even more—might be sustainable for many retirees. But the true lesson isn't the number itself.

It's this: no formula can replace a flexible, personalized strategy. The best retirement plans are living documents—responsive to inflation, market cycles, and your own evolving priorities.

So yes, take comfort that the math may allow you to spend a little more. But don't mistake a percentage point for certainty. Use it as a guidepost, not a guarantee.

Plan wisely. Review often. Stay flexible. That's how you turn a rule of thumb into a retirement plan that actually lasts.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: Bitcoin Taps $121,000 As Hot 'Uptober' Start Reboots All-Time High Predictions
data.description:
As Bitcoin (CRYPTO: BTC) tapped $121,000 on Friday morning, market commentators except the year’s final quarter to deliver positive returns for both BTC and Ethereum (CRYPTO: ETH).

What Happened: Trader Smiley Capital on Thursday pointed out that prices above $120,000 could act as a distribution zone for 2023 buyers, though he still targets $140,000–$160,000.

A drop below key support of $118,000 would invalidate this bullish thesis.

Ethereum remains largely correlated with Bitcoin, while most altcoins are still below their August exit levels, suggesting upside potential, particularly for high-beta assets.

Overall, the outlook is optimistic, with the potential for a very strong Q4 across crypto markets.

Also Read: Bitcoin, Ethereum, XRP Remain Strong As ETF Flows Reach $900 Million

What's Next: Innerdevcrypto anticipates a strong finish to the four-year cycle. His predictions:

  • Bitcoin may reach $150,000–$190,000, a roughly 10x increase from its $15,000 bottom.
  • His plans include selling 50% of BTC bought near $19,000 at cycle highs, with the intention to rebuy in about a year, following historical patterns.
  • BTC is expected to surge first, followed by majors and a brief altseason favoring well-performing coins or new narratives. Any alt rally is likely short-lived, so quick profit-taking using BTC as collateral is recommended.

He cautions that falling BTC treasury stock prices could trigger partial selling, possibly retesting the $70,000–$100,000 range, though a full -80% crash is considered unlikely.

  • CME Group To Launch 24/7 Cryptocurrency Futures Trading In 2026

Image: Shutterstock
